name: Smuggler's Cove
description: Martin Cate's three-story rum temple has been the world's most influential tiki bar since 2009 — a candlelit pirate ship where 80+ exotic cocktails span three centuries of rum history. Drinks pull from colonial taverns, Prohibition-era Havana, and the golden tiki age, built fresh to order from a 600+ rum library. The interior is pure transportive theater: rope bridges, waterfalls, flickering lanterns. No reservations, line forms early. The cult classic that put SF tiki back on the map.

name: Bourbon & Branch
description: An actual Prohibition-era speakeasy that ran illegally from 1921 to 1933, now resurrected as one of the country's most reverent classic-cocktail bars. Reservations only, password at the door, house rules forbid cell phones and standing at the bar. Tin ceilings, red velvet wallpaper, and an encyclopedic spirits selection — every bottle hand-picked for merit. The blueprint that launched the modern speakeasy revival.

name: Pagan Idol
description: A Financial District tiki temple that occupies the former bones of Tiki Bob's Mainland Rendezvous — wood totems, thatched huts, a starlit ceiling, and a volcano that erupts on schedule with fog and theatrical flair. The cocktail menu skews original-tropical with classic tiki drinks available off-menu, and the room hits a sweet spot between kitsch and craft. Drink the Volcano with a group; everyone gets pulled into the show.

name: Local Edition
description: Tucked into the basement of the historic Hearst Building, Local Edition is a newspaper-themed cocktail lounge stuffed with vintage typewriters, printing presses, and Edison-bulb glow. The progressive beverage program leans on rotating seasonal cocktails, and live jazz, soul, and DJ sets fill the space most nights. A standout late-night option for the FiDi/Mid-Market crossover crowd.
hours: Mon 4:30pm-12am; Tue-Wed 4:30pm-1am; Thu-Fri 4:30pm-2am; Sat 6pm-2am; Sun closed

name: Tonga Room & Hurricane Bar
description: An indoor tropical fantasy in the basement of the Fairmont — a former indoor swimming pool transformed into a lagoon where a floating barge hosts live music while simulated thunderstorms roll across the ceiling every 20 minutes. The Pacific Rim menu and tiki cocktails are pure spectacle, anchored by what locals will argue is the city's best Mai Tai. A $20 entertainment cover applies during live music. Pure SF showbiz.

name: The Buena Vista Cafe
description: The 1916 cable-car-stop saloon that introduced Irish Coffee to America in November 1952, when journalist Stanton Delaplane convinced owner Jack Koeppler to recreate the Shannon Airport version. Bartenders still build them in long elegant rows behind the bar, the mugs perpetually steaming. The food is honest American diner — corned beef hash, Dungeness crab omelets, Reubens — but you come for the coffee and the view of the Powell-Hyde line clattering by.

name: Vesuvio Cafe
description: The Beat Generation's living room since 1948, perched on Jack Kerouac Alley directly across from City Lights Bookstore. Two cluttered floors of stained-glass windows, peeling poster art, and the ghosts of Kerouac, Ferlinghetti, and Dylan Thomas. The cocktail menu is more curated than you'd expect from a 75-year-old bohemian dive — Averna Spritzes, the house Boulevardier, the literary 'Gin-sberg'. A place to drink slowly and read.

name: Specs' Twelve Adler Museum Cafe
description: An unmarked door off Saroyan Place in North Beach opens into 1968 — a museum-cafe of nautical curios, dusty knickknacks, faded photographs, and decades of regulars. Founded by Richard 'Specs' Simmons as a working-class union bar, it's served Beat poets, dockworkers, Thelonious Monk, and Herb Caen for over half a century. SF Legacy Business since 2016. No food, no frills, no compromise.
address: 12 William Saroyan Pl, San Francisco, CA 94133

name: Li Po Cocktail Lounge
description: Open since 1937, Li Po is a low-ceilinged Chinatown cave dimly lit by red paper lanterns and a giant gilded Buddha behind the bar. The signature Chinese Mai Tai — three rums, a 'Chinese liqueur', and a splash of pineapple — was canonized as 'lethal' by Anthony Bourdain on Parts Unknown, and the line of pilgrims hasn't slowed since. A surviving relic of WWII-era Chinatown nightclub culture, mostly unchanged for 80+ years.

name: The Devil's Acre
description: Named after the 19th-century Barbary Coast block of opium dens and pickpockets, Devil's Acre is a Future Bars cocktail apothecary on Columbus where bartenders mix tinctures, bitters, and forgotten medicinal recipes from a wall of old pharmacy bottles. Think pre-Prohibition SF in chemist coats. Two floors, deep happy hour, and a more whiskey-forward menu than its tiki neighbors.

name: Cold Drinks Bar at China Live
description: Upstairs from the China Live food hall, Cold Drinks Bar channels a 1930s Shanghai French Concession lounge — moody jade lighting, lacquered woods, hand-painted murals. The cocktail program leans on Asian botanicals, tea infusions, and rare bai jius. The 'Sometimes Old Fashioned' (lapsang-souchong-smoked, sesame oil washed) won Cocktail of the Year and put the room on global best-bars lists. Reservations strongly recommended.

name: Long Weekend
description: An immersive concept bar in a meticulously restored 1920s bank at Columbus and Broadway — every six months the entire interior, soundtrack, and cocktail menu is rebuilt around a different global destination. The debut iteration, Long Weekend Havana, runs as a full sensory journey through Cuban culture: live son montuno, environmental projection, daiquiri and mojito riffs from $14. The most ambitious bar opening of the year.

name: Last Rites
description: Billed as a 'Polynesian-Noir Adventure', Last Rites is built inside a fictional crashed 1960s airplane fuselage — actual airplane seats serve as bar chairs, banyan-tree roots crawl across the ceiling, and two nine-foot fire-breathing skull idols loom overhead. The 150-rum, theatrical cocktail program leans tropical-gothic: smoking tikis, flaming garnishes, hidden absinthe. One of the country's most committed bar concepts.
hours: Tue-Wed 5pm-11pm; Thu 5pm-1am; Fri-Sat 6pm-2am; Sun 5pm-11pm; Mon closed

name: Elixir Saloon
description: SF's second-oldest saloon site, dating to 1858 and still pouring on the same Mission corner under proprietor H. Joseph Ehrmann. The cocktail program is part history lesson, part working bar — Pisco Punch from a mid-1800s recipe, Irish Coffees, Chartreuse Swizzles, and a sliding-percentage happy hour that resets every hour. A neighborhood institution that takes craft seriously without the precious staging.

name: Lost Resort
description: A ramshackle tropical hideout off 20th and Bryant — tiki kitsch reframed as a rundown island saloon, with palm-thatched booths, found-object decor, and a kitchen turning out late-night bar food until close. More easygoing and food-forward than its tiki peers, with a backyard-leaning vibe that fills up on warm Mission nights.

name: Tartine Manufactory
description: Chad Robertson and Elisabeth Prueitt's flagship — part bakery, part coffee bar, part wood-fired sourdough-pizza restaurant — anchored by the brick oven at the heart of the converted Heath Ceramics warehouse. Counter-service mornings turn out the legendary morning bun, country loaves, and open smoked-salmon tartines; dinner pivots to sourdough pizza, full cocktails, and a globally minded wine list. The most-photographed bakery in California.

name: Verjus
description: Michael and Lindsay Tusk's (Quince, Cotogna) Parisian cave à manger, reopened in late 2024 and now the chicest seat in Jackson Square. Stand at the zinc bar with a glass of natural wine and small French plates — mussels escabeche, oeufs mentaiko with pollock roe, charred sausage stuffed with manchego. The 100+ Champagnes-under-$100 list is a wine-bar flex few rooms in town can match.

name: Starlite
description: Reborn on the 21st floor of the Beacon Grand (formerly the Sir Francis Drake), Starlite is the iconic Harry Denton's Starlight Room reimagined for 2024 — Scott Baird's cocktail program, Michelin-vet Johnny Spero's snack menu (the Hot Fried Chicken Bun is a Chinatown nod), and a 360° wraparound view of the city skyline. Vinyl DJ sets, a monthly drag brunch, and bottle service for the rare big night out.
address: 450 Powell St, 21st Floor, San Francisco, CA 94102

name: Zam Zam
description: Opened in 1941 as the Persian Aub Zam Zam by Assyrian-born Samson Mooshei, this Persian Art Deco cocktail lounge on Haight Street was famously presided over by his son Bruno — who would refuse to serve anything but a gin martini and bar customers for ordering wrong. Herb Caen called it 'the Holy Shrine of the Dry Martini'. The Persian arches, horseshoe bar, and dim red glow are essentially unchanged for 80+ years.

name: Bar Iris
description: The cocktail companion to Chef David Yoshimura's Michelin-starred Nisei, tucked into Polk Street's Russian Hill stretch. The bar program leans hard into Japanese spirits — single-cask whiskies, koshu sake, umeshu, shochu — built into seasonal cocktails with the same precision as the kitchen. Nisei sends izakaya plates over for bar guests; the result is one of the most quietly excellent rooms in the city.

name: Cavaña
description: A 17th-floor Mission Bay perch with sweeping bay views, leaning into Latin American spirits — mezcal, tequila, rum, cachaça — paired with ingredient-driven small plates. The cocktail program puts ceviches and agave at the center; the room glows pink at sunset over Oracle Park. Newer to the SF skyline-bar conversation but already a Chase Center pre-game favorite.
address: 100 Channel St, 17th Floor, San Francisco, CA 94158

name: Radhaus
description: An Alpine beer hall in Fort Mason's Building A with a postcard view of the Golden Gate Bridge. From the team behind Suppenküche and Biergarten, Radhaus pours an exceptional rotation of German pilsners, hefeweizens, and lagers alongside a modern Bavarian menu — pretzels with Haus mustard, currywurst, jägerschnitzel, käsekuchen. The communal long tables and cathedral ceilings make it one of the city's best big-group rooms.
address: 2 Marina Blvd, Building A, San Francisco, CA 94123

name: Causwells
description: Chef Adam Rosenblum's Marina bistro has been quietly running one of the country's best smash-style cheeseburger programs since 2014 — the Americana, double-patty, on a soft roll, with American cheese and house pickles, regularly tops national lists. Beyond the burger, the menu wanders into French-Spanish-Italian territory with house ricotta on lavash, '1000 Layer' potatoes, and an excellent natural-leaning wine list. A neighborhood spot with destination food.
hours: Tue-Wed 11am-9pm; Thu 11am-10pm; Fri 11am-10:30pm; Sat 10am-10:30pm; Sun 10am-9pm; Mon closed

name: Novela
description: A sprawling literary-themed cocktail lounge in SoMa where the walls are lined with chromatically arranged book collections and the cocktails are named for fictional characters — Leopold Bloom, Jay Gatsby, Jane Eyre. Bar Stars Kate Bolton and Alex Smith built the program around handcrafted classics and a historically inspired Punch-on-Tap rotation. Big enough to absorb a corporate group without losing the ambiance.
hours: Mon 4pm-12am; Tue-Wed 4pm-1am; Thu-Fri 4pm-2am; Sat 5pm-2am; Sun closed
